coredata.dc:title: A dual-strategy for the valorization and regeneration of spent activated carbon
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Wenjing
Qiao, Zihang
Wang, Bin
Li, Zheyu
Zhang, Minghui
coredata.dc:description: To accomplish the objective of reusing spent activated carbon (AC), this study achieved the in-situ regeneration of spent AC through a chemical oxidation process that produced carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The primary focus was to investigate the physical and chemical structural transformations occurring during the conversion of AC into CQDs, elucidating the in-situ regeneration mechanism of the spent AC. The ultimate aim was to develop a durable, efficient, and cost-effective activated carbon adsorbent. The findings indicate that the in-situ regenerated AC recovered 68 % of its original adsorption capacity during CQDs (with a quantum yield of 8 %) preparation process. Notably, the regenerated AC displayed a distinctive hierarchical petal-like structure and an increase in surface oxygenated functional groups, which shifted its adsorption mechanism from predominantly physical to chemical adsorption. Furthermore, the incorporation of the TiO2 modifier reduced the band gap of the AC/CQDs to 2.13 eV, thus broadening the composite system's response spectrum under visible light and decreasing the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Consequently, the in-situ regeneration of spent AC was enhanced and superior to that reported in existing literature.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Spent activated carbon
Carbon quantum dots
TiO2
Photocatalytic regeneration
Resource recycling

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CQDs. The illustration shows the particle size distribution of CQDs. (b) Ultraviolet spectrogram of CQDs. In the illustration, the left is a picture of CQDs solution in visible light, and the right is a picture of CQDS solution in 365 nm ultraviolet light. (c) Photoluminescent emission spectra of CQDs at different excitation wavelengths. (d-f) Effects of different factors on fluorescence properties of CQDs: (d) Stirring reflux time; (e) Oil bath temperature; (f) Molar concentration of nitric acid; (g) Types of passivating agents.

(a-d) SEM diagram of samples: (a) AC; (b) AC/TiO2; (c) AC/CQDs; (d) AC/CQDs/1.0TiO2. (e) XRD pattern of samples. (f) Nitrogen absorption and desorption curve of samples. (g) Pore size distribution of samples.
(a) UV-VIS diffuse reflection spectrum of samples; (b) Band gap spectra of samples; (c) Fluorescence spectrum of samples.
Fitting diagram of C1s, O1s, Ti2p, N1s discrimination peaks of samples.

a) The effect of initial concentration on adsorption properties; b) The effect of adsorption time on adsorption properties; c) The effect of pH value on adsorption properties; d) The effect of temperature on adsorption properties; e) The effect of Na+ concentration on adsorption properties; f) Effect of Mg2+ concentration on adsorption properties.

(a) Effect of 100 mg/L methylene blue on regeneration performance; (b) The effect of 200 mg/L methylene blue on the regeneration performance; (c) The effect of 300 mg/L methylene blue dye on sample regeneration performance; (d) The effect of pH value on regeneration performance.
Photocatalytic regeneration mechanism diagram of AC/CQDs/1.0TiO2.
Specific surface area (m2/g)and pore volume (cm3/g)of samples.
Elemental quantitative analysis and the percentage content of carbon and oxygen functional groups.

head.ce:title: A dual-strategy for the valorization and regeneration of spent activated carbon

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Art Design, Inner Mongolia Agricultural University, Hohhot, 010018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Art Design

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Art Design, Inner Mongolia Agricultural University, Hohhot, 010018, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Mongolia Key Laboratory of Sandy Shrubs Fibrosis and Energy Development and Utilization, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Mongolia Key Laboratory of Sandy Shrubs Fibrosis and Energy Development and Utilization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Mongolia Key Laboratory of Sandy Shrubs Fibrosis and Energy Development and Utilization

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Materials Science and Art Design, Inner Mongolia Agricultural University, Hohhot 010018, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Art Design

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To accomplish the objective of reusing spent activated carbon (AC), this study achieved the in-situ regeneration of spent AC through a chemical oxidation process that produced carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The primary focus was to investigate the physical and chemical structural transformations occurring during the conversion of AC into CQDs, elucidating the in-situ regeneration mechanism of the spent AC. The ultimate aim was to develop a durable, efficient, and cost-effective activated carbon adsorbent. The findings indicate that the in-situ regenerated AC recovered 68 % of its original adsorption capacity during CQDs (with a quantum yield of 8 %) preparation process. Notably, the regenerated AC displayed a distinctive hierarchical petal-like structure and an increase in surface oxygenated functional groups, which shifted its adsorption mechanism from predominantly physical to chemical adsorption. Furthermore, the incorporation of the TiO2 modifier reduced the band gap of the AC/CQDs to 2.13 eV, thus broadening the composite system's response spectrum under visible light and decreasing the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Consequently, the in-situ regeneration of spent AC was enhanced and superior to that reported in existing literature.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors declare that all data supporting the findings of this study are available within the article and from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: Activated carbon (AC) is highly acclaimed for its porosity, extensive surface area, and remarkable adsorption properties, especially in the elimination of organic contaminants from industrial wastewater [1,2]. Nevertheless, a significant constraint of AC in practical applications is its propensity for adsorption saturation. Once the adsorption capacity is exhausted, the saturated AC is considered as waste, possibly containing non-volatile organic compounds and frequently labeled as hazardous [3]. It is documented that the global production of hazardous waste annually reaches 330 million t, with spent AC adsorbents accounting for a substantial 65 % of this total [4]. Furthermore, numerous studies have revealed that while spent AC can be regenerated via thermal regeneration, biological regeneration, solvent extraction, electrochemical processes, and photocatalytic treatments [5], these methods are fraught with challenges such as low regeneration efficiencies, substantial energy expenditure, structural damage to AC, subpar quality of the recycled material, and limited application scope. Consequently, the development of a straightforward, effective, energy-efficient, and eco-friendly treatment method for spent AC possesses considerable scientific relevance and practical utility.

Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs), as an emergent zero-dimensional material, hold immense promise in various domains, including photocatalysis, bioimaging, sensing, and fluorescent probing [6], owing to their exceptional chemical robustness, biocompatibility, water solubility, and low toxicity [7]. The transformation of spent AC into value-added CQDs not only epitomizes the adage “turning waste into treasure”, but also obviates the need for carbon precursor synthesis, presenting a novel paradigm for the efficient utilization of spent AC. However, the conversion rate of AC to CQDs remains low, with a quantum yield of only 2 % to 8 %. This inefficiency results in a significant amount of unconverted AC being discarded without full utilization [8]. During the conversion process, the physicochemical structure of AC is likely to undergo changes. However, the extent of these changes in restoring or enhancing the adsorption capacity of AC has not been thoroughly studied in the literature. Additionally, the separation of unconverted AC from CQDs is complex, typically involving processes such as centrifugation and purification. These steps not only complicate the preparation of CQDs but also increase associated costs. Therefore, this paper will focus on studying the changes in the physical and chemical structure of AC during its transformation into CQDs, revealing the mechanism of its in-situ regeneration, as well as the performance of AC/CQDs, especially its adsorption performance and photocatalytic degradation performance, in order to utilize the photocatalytic characteristics of CQDs.

Given the limitations of a single CQDs chemical structure, such as a narrower light absorption range and a higher recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, its photocatalytic activity is not satisfactory [9]. This paper introduces TiO2 into the AC/CQDs composite system. Under UV light excitation, TiO2 can effectively promote the separation of electron-hole pairs, while CQDs, with their excellent electron transfer performance, can accelerate the migration rate of electrons and holes in the composite system under specific conditions [10], thereby improving the redox efficiency of photocatalysis and enhancing the photocatalytic activity of the composite material.

Spent AC is derived from saturated AC that has adsorbed dye wastewater, with Salix cheilophila serving as the primary feedstock. Titanium tetrachloride (TiCl4) was acquired from Shanghai Maclin Biochemical Co., Ltd. and is of analytical purity. Methylene blue (MB) was sourced from Tianjin Nankai Yungong Synthesis Technology Co., Ltd. All other reagents employed were commercially available and certified to be of analytical grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs derived from spent AC
ce:section.ce:para: 2 g spent AC powder were introduced into 200 mL of HNO3 solutions with varying molar concentrations (2 mol/L, 4 mol/L, 6 mol/L, 8 mol/L), which were subsequently stirred at a rate of 500 rpm. The stirring process was conducted for 8 h, 10 h, 12 h, and 14 h, respectively, with the reflux temperature meticulously controlled at 80 °C, 120 °C, 160 °C, and 200 °C, respectively. Following natural cooling to ambient temperature, the suspension was carefully decanted and subjected to distillation under pressure at 150 °C for 3 h until the residual solution was dried to a powder form. Subsequently, 100 mL of 0.5 mol/L sodium hydroxide solution was introduced into the distillation flask containing the neutralized solution. The resulting black suspension was collected and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min to eliminate the non-fluorescent precipitate. The supernatant was then filtered through a 0.22 μm filter to yield a dark brown CQDs solution. 1 g of a passivating agent (either urea or polyethylene glycol 1500) was added to the CQDs aqueous solution, which was then passivated at 120 °C for 72 h The solution was subsequently purified in a Tris buffer solution for 24 h, during which the buffer was replaced four times to ensure the comprehensive removal of a substantial number of ions from the solution.

2 g of spent AC were integrated into a 4 mol/L HNO3 solution, which was then subjected to reflux for a duration of 10 h at 160 °C. Following the reflux process, the mixture was allowed to naturally cool to ambient temperature. The suspension was subsequently extracted and subjected to vacuum distillation for drying. Once the pH was adjusted to a neutral level using 100 mL of 0.5 mol/L sodium hydroxide solution, 1 g of urea was introduced into the AC/CQDs composite solution. The composite solution was then passivated at 120 °C for a period of 72 h. The suspension following the passivation process was dried to yield a yellowish brown solid, which represents the AC/CQDs composite material.

Utilizing TiCl4 as the precursor, TiCl4 solutions of varying volumes (0.5 mL, 1.0 mL, 1.5 mL, 2.0 mL, 2.5 mL) were meticulously added to the AC/CQDs mixture under intense agitation. The admixture was then introduced into a hydrothermal reactor lined with polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE), where the hydrothermal reaction was conducted at 140 °C for a duration of 6 h. Upon cooling the solution to ambient temperature, the pH was adjusted to a neutral level, and the AC/CQDs/TiO2 composite material was obtained by drying the solution at 60 °C. The reaction chemical equation of TiCl4 converted into TiO2 by hydrothermal method was expressed by Reaction (1). The prepared material was designated as AC/CQDs/nTiO2 (where n represents the incremental addition of TiCl4). The fabrication process is depicted in Fig. 1 . For comparative purposes, AC/TiO2 was prepared by hydrothermal reaction by directly blending 1.0 mL of TiCl4 with the spent AC.
(1)
TiCl 4
+
x + 2
H 2
O →
TiO 2
·
xH 2
O + 4HCl

A total of 100 mL of MB, with a concentration of 50 mg/L, was introduced into a thermostatic oscillator, to which 0.03 g of the sample was subsequently added. The oscillator was set to agitate at a rate of 40 rpm for a duration of 12 h to achieve adsorption equilibrium. Following a 5 min settling period, 4 mL of the solution were extracted using a syringe and filtered through a filter with a diameter of 25 mm and a pore size of 0.22 μm. The absorbance of the filtered solution was quantified using a UV-VIS spectrophotometer at an absorption wavelength of 664 nm. The effects of pH, the initial concentration of the dye solution, the duration of adsorption, the temperature of adsorption, and the concentration of heteroions on the adsorption characteristics of the composite were systematically investigated. The method for calculating the adsorption capacity is detailed in Eq. (2).
(2)
q e
=
C 0
−
C 1
× V
M

In the formula,
q e
is the adsorption capacity of dye solution(mg/g);
C 0
is the concentration of the dye solution before adsorption(mg/L);
C 1
is the concentration of the dye solution after adsorption(mg/L); V is the volume of the dye solution (L); M is the amount of composite material.

100 mL MB, with a concentration of 50 mg/L, was meticulously combined with 0.03 g of the sample within a temperature-controlled oscillating chamber. The mixture was agitated at 25 °C in the absence of light for a duration of 24 h to achieve adsorption saturation. Following a 3 min settling period, 4 mL of the solution were carefully withdrawn using a syringe and filtered through a filter membrane with a diameter of 25 mL and a pore size of 0.22 μm. The absorbance of the filtered solution was quantitatively determined at 664 nm. Subsequently, the Xe lamp (500 W) was activated as the light source to initiate the photocatalytic reaction. At predetermined intervals of irradiation, 4 mL of the solution were extracted and filtered in the same manner to measure its absorbance. The calculated results were utilized to evaluate the regeneration efficiency of the sample. The effects of pH and the initial concentration of the dye solution on the photocatalytic regeneration performance of the composites were thoroughly investigated. The regeneration performance of the sample over the 24–36 h period was calculated in accordance with Formula (3):
(3)
Regeneration performance =
A t
/
A 24

In the formula, A
t is the absorbance of MB at t time, A
24 is the absorbance of 24 h MB solution.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM-2100, Japan) was employed to scrutinize the morphology of CQDs. Concurrently, scanning electron microscopy (SEM, SU-8020, Japan) was utilized to discern the apparent morphology of each sample. An X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D8Advance, Germany) was utilized to analyze the crystal structure of the samples. A fluorescence spectrometer (PL, FLS1000, UK) was employed to assess the steady-state luminescence of the samples. An ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (UV–Vis, TU-1950, China) was utilized to measure the ultraviolet spectrum and band gap width of the samples. An X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, ThermoEXCALAB250Xi, USA) and fourier-transform infrared spectra (FTIR, IS10, Nicolet, USA) were utilized to probe the surface elements and functional groups of the samples. The pore structure of the sample was evaluated using a physical adsorption instrument (ASAP-2460, USA).

TEM imaging, as depicted in Fig. 2(a), revealed the absence of discernible lattice fringes in the CQDs, suggesting that the CQDs synthesized in this investigation consist of amorphous carbon. Utilizing particle size distribution calculation software, the diameters of 200 CQDs nanoparticles were quantified, demonstrating a relatively homogeneous particle size distribution with an average diameter of 3.87 nm, predominantly ranging between 1.47 nm to 8.93 nm.

UV-VIS spectroscopic characterization elucidated the CQDs' robust UV absorption capabilities, as illustrated in Fig. 2(b). The CQDs aqueous solution exhibits a marked absorption peak at 225 nm, which can be attributed to the π-π* transition in aromatic CC bonds [11]. Meanwhile, the distinctive absorption peak around 280 nm is primarily associated with the π-π* absorption band of the aromatic sp2 carbonaceous structures within the CQDs, indicative of the presence of aromatic conjugation. Furthermore, a weak absorption peak is observed around 360 nm, corresponding to the n-σ* and n-π* transitions of nitrogen-containing functional groups [12]. The illustration in Fig. 2(b) demonstrates the CQDs' excellent water dispersibility. The CQDs aqueous solution appears transparent and dark brown under natural lighting, whereas under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, it exhibits a vivid yellow fluorescence.

Fig. 2(c) illustrates that as the excitation wavelength is elevated from 280 nm to 400 nm, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs aqueous solution diminishes progressively, with no discernible redshift or blueshift in the emission wavelengths corresponding to the varying excitation wavelengths. These findings imply that the CQDs synthesized in this study exhibit negligible excitation wavelength dependence [13]. This phenomenon can be attributed to the abundant amino groups present on the surface of the CQDs following passivation. Substances containing amino groups can serve as efficient surface passivators, regulating the surface state of the CQDs and inhibiting their excitation-dependent photoluminescent properties. A decrease in the amino content on the surface of the CQDs results in a lower degree of passivation, leading to excitation-dependent fluorescence characteristics. Conversely, a higher amino content on the CQDs surface leads to complete passivation, manifesting as excitation independence. Consequently, reducing the amino content can enhance the excitation-dependent fluorescence characteristics of the CQDs to a certain extent [14].

Fig. 2(d-g) delineates the impact of diverse fabrication protocols on the photoluminescence intensity of CQDs. With the duration of reflux extending from 8 h to 12 h, the photoluminescence intensity of CQDs initially ascends and then descends. This is attributable to the continuous augmentation in reflux duration, which initiates further transformations or polymerizations of the formed products, thereby diminishing the photoluminescence intensity [15]. As the synthesis temperature escalates from 80 °C to 200 °C, the photoluminescence intensity of CQDs also demonstrates an initial increase followed by a decline. This can be ascribed to the exorbitant temperature interfering with the formation of carbon nuclei, resulting in the aggregation of CQDs and partial photoluminescence quenching [16]. With the escalation of HNO3 molarity, the photoluminescence intensity of CQDs initially augments and then attenuates gradually. When the HNO3 molarity reaches 4 mol/L, the photoluminescence intensity of the fabricated CQDs is at its zenith. This is due to the insufficient oxidation degree of AC at low acid concentrations, which restricts the number of functional groups introduced onto the CQDs' surface and thereby hampers the enhancement of their photoluminescent properties. Conversely, an excessively high acid concentration leads to excessive oxidation, which not only disintegrates the CQDs' structure, increases non-radiative recombination centers, but also results in CQDs of diminutive size, impeding their quantum confinement effect and subsequently diminishing the photoluminescence intensity [17]. Passivation bolsters the photoluminescence intensity of CQDs, as the surface of CQDs harbors defects that can be stabilized by passivating agents to augment the luminescence [18]. In Fig. 2 (g), urea significantly enhances the photoluminescence intensity of CQDs, while polyethylene glycol marginally augments it. Following passivation, the position of the CQDs' peak photoluminescence emission remains unaltered. The superior effect of urea is primarily attributed to its molecular constitution, which encompasses multiple amino and hydroxyl functional groups that chemically interact with the surface groups of CQDs to form a more stable surface modification layer, thereby bolstering the photoluminescence performance of CQDs. Furthermore, urea's commendable solubility facilitates thorough mixing and reaction with CQDs during the preparation process, enhancing the efficacy of surface modification [19].

Based on the aforementioned findings, the optimal synthesis conditions for CQDs are determined as follows: the stirring reflux time is set at 10 h, the reaction temperature (in an oil bath) is maintained at 160 °C, the molar concentration of nitric acid is fixed at 4 mol/L, and urea is employed as the passivating agent. The highest quantum yield of CQDs under these conditions is computed using Eq. (3), yielding a value of 8.1 %, which exceeds the quantum yield reported in the literature [20]. The quantum yield is calculated in accordance with Eq. (4).
(4)
Y 1
=
Y 2
S 1
S 2
A 2
A 1
n 2 1
n 2 2

In the formula, subscripts 1 and 2 respectively represent unknown objects to be measured and reference standard objects. Y
is the fluorescence quantum yield, S
is the fluorescence peak area, A is absorbance, n is the refractive index of solvent (Because the concentration of unknown substance to be measured and reference standard substance in this experiment is very low, the influence of solute can be ignored, and they are regarded as consistent, both being 1.33.).

SEM depicted in Fig. 3 (a-d) revealed that the cross-section of unused AC for dye solution adsorption was smooth, with the original morphology of Salix wood cells preserved. As illustrated in Fig. 3(b), AC/TiO2 prepared via the hydrothermal method also essentially maintained the original shape of the Salix wood cells, with TiO2 adhering to the AC surface in the form of white particles. Fig. 3(c) illustrates that the original cell structure of AC is disrupted during the HNO3 oxidation process, leading to the formation of a petal-like hierarchical structure and spherical solid particles. This petal-like hierarchical structure is uncommon in CQDs prepared from other materials and can be attributed to the fact that AC retains the cell morphology of the original Salix wood. During the oxidation process, the carbon source within the AC cell wall is removed layer by layer, resulting in the thinning of the wall layer to form a petal-like structure, while the stripped carbon source accumulates to form granular solids. As depicted in Fig. 3(d), upon TiO2 loading, the petal-like hierarchical structure and spherical solid particles persist, with the wall layer of the hierarchical structure becoming thicker, suggesting that a portion of TiO2 is attached to the wall layer in the form of thin films. Additionally, a small amount of TiO2 adheres to the surface and interior of the hierarchical structure of the AC as white particles.

In Fig. 3(e), unused AC exhibits two broad diffraction peaks near 2θ = 23.5° and 43.9°, respectively, which correspond to the disordered graphite 002 and 100 (overlapping 100 and 101) diffraction peaks [21], indicating that AC possesses a disordered graphitized structure. AC/TiO2 shows distinct anatase TiO2 crystal diffraction peaks near 2θ = 25.3°, 38.1°, 48.1°, 54.3°, 55.0°, and 62.8° [22]. Following HNO3 oxidation, the disordered graphitized structure of AC is disrupted, hence its characteristic peak is absent in the AC/CQDs diagram. Typically, diffraction peaks in the 2θ = 18°-22° range correspond to CQDs [23]; nevertheless, due to their high dispersion, minuscule size, and relatively faint diffraction intensity, no characteristic peak of CQDs is discernible in the AC/CQDs diagram. New diffraction peaks are observed at 2θ = 31.9° and 47.6°, which are attributed to the formation of methylformamide crystals [24], likely a result of further hydrolysis of the urea passivator during the hydrothermal process. The reemergence of the anatase TiO2 crystal diffraction peak upon addition of TiCl4 confirms the successful support of TiO2.

The N2 adsorption curves of unused AC and AC/TiO2 in Fig. 3(f) align with Type I adsorption curves [25], suggesting that these samples are primarily composed of micropores [26]. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of other samples exhibit minimal adsorption capacity, accompanied by an adsorption-desorption hysteresis loop, indicative of capillary condensation and the presence of mesopores. Table 1 enumerates the specific surface area and pore volume values for each sample. Given that AC is the unused sample for dye solution adsorption, it possesses the largest specific surface area and pore volume. Despite the reduction in porosity upon TiO2 support, the AC/TiO2 sample still demonstrates a higher porosity than the other samples. The specific surface area and pore volume of AC/CQDs samples are exceedingly low due to the destruction of the micropore structure of AC by HNO3 oxidation. When TiO2 is loaded onto the AC/CQDs sample, it is evident that both the specific surface area and pore volume of the AC/CQDs/TiO2 sample increase with the increase in TiO2 loading capacity, attributed to the mesoporous nature of the hydrothermally prepared TiO2, which augments the specific surface area and pore volume [27]. As depicted in Fig. 3(g), the AC/TiO2 samples exhibit two distribution peaks in the 0–2 nm micropore range and the 2–6 nm mesoporous range, confirming the mesoporous nature of the synthesized TiO2. The pore size distribution peak of AC/CQDs is centered in the mesoporous range of 20 nm. With TiO2 loading, the pore range of AC/CQDs/TiO2 shifts to the left, predominantly distributing in the mesoporous range of 2–20 nm, and the pore range of 2-7 nm expands with increasing TiO2 loading.

As depicted in Fig. 4(a), the absorption peak of AC/CQDs is situated at approximately 312 nm, which can be attributed to the nanoscale size and quantum size effect of CQDs, conferring upon them a heightened absorption capacity for ultraviolet light. Nonetheless, their absorption capacity is relatively weak within the visible light spectrum. AC/TiO2 exhibits absorption peaks at 237 nm, 293 nm, 375 nm, and 480 nm, respectively; the appearance of absorption peak in the visible range is attributed to carbon doping of TiO2. The UV absorption peak of AC/CQDs/1.0TiO2 exhibits a distinct redshift in comparison to AC/TiO2 and AC/CQDs, with the wavelength corresponding to the maximum absorbance at 481 nm. These findings corroborate that the AC-CQDs-TiO2 triplex significantly expands the visible light response spectrum of the composite. The aforementioned phenomenon can be further elucidated by referring to Eq. (5):
(5)
α hv
1 2
= A
hv −
E g

In the formula, α is the absorbance, A is the absorption constant of the indirect transition, hv is the photon energy, and E
g is the band gap energy. Take (αhv)1/2 as the ordinate and hv as the abscissa.

As shown in Fig. 4(b), the band gap value of AC/TiO2 is 2.59 eV, that of AC/CQDs is 2.73 eV, and that of AC/CQDs/1.0TiO2 is only 2.13 eV, all of which is significantly lower than that of pure TiO2 (3.2 eV) [28].The band gap value of AC/CQDs/1.0TiO2 is the smallest, which can be inferred that the visible light catalytic activity is higher than that of AC/TiO2 and AC/CQDs.

In general, the intensity of PL spectrum peak is directly proportional to the extent of electron-hole recombination. As depicted in Fig. 4(c), the AC/CQDs composite exhibit two distinct emission peaks within the wavelength range of 400–500 nm and 550–650 nm, with the most intense peaks occurring at 442 nm and 603 nm, respectively. The former emission peak can be attributed to the predominance of the sp2 carbon hybridization region, with a greater hybridization area corresponding to a stronger peak intensity [29]. For the latter emission peaks within the 550–650 nm range, their occurrence is a result of the specific structural features of CQDs, particularly the presence of a substantial number of oxygen-containing functional groups [30]. In the case of AC/TiO2, a predominant emission peak emerges within the 400–500 nm range, with its peak situates at approximately 437 nm, which is attributed to the excitation of electrons within TiO2 itself [31].Comparatively, the emission peak position of the AC/CQDs/1.0TiO2 sample exhibits minimal shift from the other two samples, yet the PL spectral peak intensity is notably diminished, suggesting that the photoinduced electron-hole recombination within the AC/CQDs/1.0TiO2 sample is effectively suppressed, thus indicating a high photocatalytic activity. When comparing AC/CQDs with the other two samples, it is observed that AC/CQDs exhibits a subtle redshift in the peak position within the 400–500 nm range. This redshift may result from the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups in AC/CQDs. These functional groups alter electron distribution and decrease the energy gap between energy levels, leading to the formation of distinct surface defects, which ultimately contribute to the observed redshift. [32]. Furthermore, the PL spectral peak intensity of AC/CQDs is the most pronounced, indicating that the photoinduced electron-hole recombination efficiency of AC/CQDs surpassed that of the other two samples, that is not conducive to enhancing photocatalytic efficiency.

Table 2 presents the quantitative analysis of XPS elements. The atomic percentage of element C is the most abundant in the unused AC samples, followed by element O, N，and P. This compositional profile aligns with the conventional makeup of AC. The inclusion of a small amount of element N is attributed to the raw material of Salix wood, which inherently contains trace amounts of element N. The presence of element P is due to the H3PO4 activator used in the AC preparation. For CQDs and AC/CQDs synthesized using spent AC as the feedstock, no new elements are introduced, but the atomic percentage of element C decreases, while the atomic percentages of element O and N increases significantly. The atomic percentage of element P remained almost unchanged. This shift is attributed to the oxidation reaction with HNO3, which endows CQDs with a plethora of oxygen-containing functional groups. The rise in N atomic percentage is attributed to the use of urea as a passivator. Upon loading TiO2, element Ti is detected, and the atomic percentage of element C decreases, while the atomic percentages of element O and P increase. The increase in element P is ascribed to the TiO2's fixing effect on phosphorus. Compared to AC/CQDs, the atomic percentages of element O and N in AC/CQDs/1.0TiO2 decreased, the P remained unchanged, and the atomic percentages of element C and Ti increased markedly. This discrepancy may be attributed to the additional hydrothermal treatment of the AC/CQDs sample, which results in the further decomposition of surface functional groups. Simultaneously, residual urea is further decomposed into nitrogen-containing intermediates, which reduces the nitrogen content [33].

To delve deeper into the surface functional group composition and the valence states of each element within the sample, peak fitting was conducted on the high-resolution spectra of C1s, O1s, Ti2p, and N1s, and AC/CQDs/1.0TiO2 was selected as a representative example, as depicted in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5(a) presents the C1s spectrum, which displays an asymmetrical curve and can be segmented into four peaks: graphitic carbon (C-C/C=C, binding energy (BE) = 284.3–284.5 eV); carbon within hydroxyl groups (CO, BE = 286.3 eV); carbon within carbonyl or quinine groups (C=O/C=N, BE = 288.1 eV); and carbon within carboxyl groups (O=C-OH, BE = 291.4 eV). The binding energy of the TiC bond is 282 eV [34], and no peak is observed at this energy, suggesting that the oxygen in the TiO2 lattice is not replaced by carbon.

Fig. 5(b) illustrates the Ti2p spectrum, which exhibits two spin-split peaks: Ti2p1/2 (BE = 464.5 eV) and Ti2p2/3 (BE = 458.8 eV). The binding energy difference between these peaks is 5.7 eV, attributed to the coupling between electron orbital motion and spin motion. This configuration indicates that Ti exists in the form of Ti4+ (TiO2) [35], with no peaks observed at 455 eV (Ti2+) and 456.7 eV (Ti3+) [36]. The absence of a peak corresponding to the CTi bond in both the Ti2p and C1s spectra indicates that no CTi bond was formed between AC/CQDs and TiO2, suggesting a connection through physical adsorption or a Ti-O-C bond [37].

Fig. 5(c) shows the O1s spectrum, which can be categorized into three peaks: the TiO group (BE = 529.8–530.4 eV) is a surface hydroxyl group binding with Ti atoms, which exhibits a shift to high binding energy, revealing that Ti3+ (oroxygen vacancy) was formed in TiO2 lattice [38]; oxygen within carboxyl, aldehyde, and ketone groups (ON, OH, and OC, BE = 531.3–531.5 eV); and oxygen within hydroxyl groups (OC, BE = 532.4–532.9 eV). These oxygen-containing functional groups enhance the dispersibility of AC/CQDs/1.0TiO2 samples in aqueous solutions and facilitate the degradation of organic pollutants. Additionally, hydroxyl groups play a pivotal role in photocatalysis and adsorption, as they can react with light to form hydroxyl free radicals (•OH) for pollutant degradation and serve as adsorption sites for pollutant molecules. Samples rich in hydroxyl groups exhibit superior regeneration performance.

The N1s spectral curve depicted in Fig. 5(d) can be parsed into four distinct peaks: Ti-O-N (BE = 400.1 eV); negative ion nitrogen (Anionic N−, BE = 401.1 eV); amide nitrogen (Amide N, BE = 399.3 eV); and amino nitrogen (Amino N, BE = 398.5 eV) [39]. The emergence of amino nitrogen is ascribed to the subsequent hydrolysis of the urea passivator, whereas the detection of amide nitrogen corroborates the interaction between the amino group within the urea passivator and the carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs, leading to the formation of amide bonds. The absence of a peak corresponding to the TiN bond at 396 eV suggests that the oxygen atom within the TiO2 lattice remains unsubstituted [40]. Both the interstitial state N (Ti-O-N) and the substitutional state N are capable of contributing to the formation of a novel intermediate energy state, thereby diminishing the band gap of TiO2 [41]. However, it is observed that the band gap reduction achieved through the interstitial N is more pronounced than that of the substitutional N, with the interstitial state N being the predominant component within the N1s spectrum. This finding underscores the significant enhancement of the photocatalytic efficacy of the AC/CQDs/1.0TiO2 samples under visible light illumination.

Table 2 enumerates the outcomes of XPS peak fitting. According to the C1s fitting results, the content of graphitic carbon (CC) diminishes, whereas the content of CO and CO increases significantly in the samples subjected to chemical oxidation of CQDs. This is due to the oxidation of graphitic carbon by HNO3 during the preparation process, leading to the formation of a greater abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups. Moreover, a portion of the AC remains unconverted during the preparation process, resulting in a higher graphitic carbon content in AC/CQDs compared to the CQDs samples. The support of TiO2 also contributes to a reduction in the graphitic carbon content and an increase in the content of CO, CO, and C=O-OH. This phenomenon can be attributed to the oxidation of graphitic carbon during the process of TiO2 support, as TiO2 reacts with water to generate potent oxidizing hydroxyl radicals. Part of the graphitic carbon reacts with these hydroxyl radicals, thereby elevating the content of oxygen-containing functional groups. This result is further corroborated by the FTIR characterization findings (see section S1, supplementary materials). The O1s fitting results indicate that following HNO3 oxidation, the content of O=C/O-H/O-N increases, while the content of OC decreases. This suggests that a multitude of oxygen-containing functional groups, such as carboxyl groups, are formed during the oxidation of spent AC, while the content of hydroxyl groups is relatively diminished. When TiO2 is supported by AC and AC/CQDs, the OTi bond becomes the predominant component of the O1s spectrum, and the contents of O=C/O-H/O-N and OC decrease. This signifies that the OTi bond plays a pivotal role in the photocatalytic reaction of AC/CQDs/1.0TiO2.

As depicted in Fig. 6(a), at low concentrations, the adsorption capacity of AC/CQDs is the largest, and that of AC/TiO2 is the smallest. This discrepancy can be attributed to the formation of more surface functional groups and defect sites on the oxidized spent AC, which in turn generate additional active adsorption sites, thereby enhancing the adsorption efficacy. Additionally, CQDs possess a diminutive size, a high surface energy, and a superior adsorption capacity, enabling them to adsorb target substances with greater efficacy [42]. At low concentrations, the adsorption capacity of AC/CQDs/TiO2 tends to decrease with the increase in the addition of TiCl4. This is because an increment in TiCl4 content might lead to the coverage of more active sites on the sample's surface by TiO2, thereby reducing the number of sites available for adsorption and subsequently diminishing the adsorption capacity of the sample. At high concentrations, the adsorption capacities of AC/CQDs and AC/CQDs/0.5TiO2 are comparable and high, whereas the capacities of other samples are similar. As the initial concentration of the MB dye solution increases, the adsorption capacity of each sample shows an upward trend. This enhancement is attributed to the increasing concentration gradient of MB, which amplifies the mass transfer driving force. Furthermore, the increased MB concentration leads to more opportunities for contact with the active sites on the adsorbent's surface, thereby enhancing the adsorption capacity. As illustrated in Fig. 6(b), with the elongation of the adsorption time, the adsorption capacity of each sample evolves from a state of “rapid adsorption” to “slow adsorption” and eventually to “adsorption equilibrium.” Initially, within the first 4 h, the samples exhibit rapid adsorption, as a result of the abundance of available adsorption sites on the surface of each sample at the onset of the adsorption process. These sites exhibit a high affinity for the molecules within the MB dye solution, allowing MB molecules to swiftly locate and adhere to the adsorbent's surface. As time progresses, the number of available adsorption sites diminishes, resulting in a slower adsorption rate, and MB molecules require more time to encounter the remaining accessible sites [43]. Within the subsequent 13–24 h, the adsorption capacity of each sample scarcely increases, as most available adsorption sites are occupied by MB molecules. At this juncture, the adsorption capacity growth almost ceases, and the adsorption process reaches an equilibrium state. As depicted in Fig. 6(c), the adsorption capacity of all samples under alkaline conditions surpasses that under acidic conditions. This can be ascribed to the fact that MB is a cationic dye. Under alkaline conditions, the dissociation degree of hydrogen ions from the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the composite material increases, resulting in a heightened electronegativity of the sample, and consequently, the electrostatic attraction between the composite material and MB cations also intensifies [44].As illustrated in Fig. 6(d), with the augmentation of the ambient temperature from 25 °C to 55 °C, the adsorption quantity of MB dye for each sample demonstrates an upward trend. This can be attributed to the reduction in solution viscosity and the decrease in mass transfer resistance as temperature increases. Furthermore, the diffusion rate of MB dye within the outer boundary layer of each sample and the internal pores of the particles also increases, facilitating the ion exchange and adsorption of the endothermic reaction. As depicted in Fig. 6(e-f), the sample's adsorption performance remains relatively invariant when the concentration of Na+ and Mg2+ is at 0.02 mol/L, but experiences a moderate decline when the ion concentration is elevated to 0.2 mol/L. This is because, at low ion concentrations (such as 0.02 mol/L), the competition between Na+, Mg2+, and MB for adsorption sites is minimal, resulting in only negligible changes in adsorption performance. Conversely, as the ion concentration escalates to 0.2 mol/L, an increased number of ions compete with MB for available adsorption sites, leading to a reduction in the quantity of MB adsorbed and a subsequent decline in the adsorption performance. Furthermore, it was observed that Mg2+ exerted a more pronounced influence on the adsorption properties than Na+.

As depicted in Fig. 7 (a-c), the regeneration efficacy of AC/CQDs is the most deficient following visible light irradiation, because CQDs has the low photo-generated electron transmission efficiency. AC/CQDs/1.0TiO2 demonstrates the optimal regeneration efficacy, as the excessive addition of TiCl4 may lead to TiO2 particle aggregation, impeding light penetration to the AC/CQDs surface and subsequently diminishing light absorption efficiency. Moreover, excessive TiCl4 may also foster an augmentation in TiO2 photogenerated electron-hole recombination, thereby reducing the photocatalytic efficiency [45]. The regeneration effect of AC/TiO2 falls short of that of AC/CQDs/1.0TiO2 but exceeds that of AC/CQDs, underscoring the superior photocatalytic performance of the heterogeneous structure comprising CQDs and TiO2 over TiO2 alone. Furthermore, in light of the pore structure analysis presented in 2.2.1, it is evident that the pore structure of AC/TiO2 is significantly more developed than that of all other samples, which facilitates the synergistic effects of adsorption and photocatalytic degradation. Consequently, the regeneration efficacy of AC/TiO2 surpasses that of AC/CQDs/0.5TiO2, AC/CQDs/1.5TiO2, AC/CQDs/2.0TiO2, and AC/CQDs/2.5TiO2. Moreover, as evidenced in Fig. 7(a-c), the photocatalytic regeneration efficacy of each sample diminishes with the escalation of the initial concentration of MB dye solution. This is because the increased concentration of MB dye solution reduces the amount of visible light that can penetrate into the solution, thereby limiting its interaction with the sample. With the extension of visible light exposure duration, the curve in Fig. 7(a-c) can be segmented into three phases. In the initial stage (rapid decline period), the photocatalytic degradation rate of all samples is at its peak, as the MB adsorbed on the photocatalyst's surface is concentrated and readily undergoes photocatalytic degradation. In the intermediate stage, the MB concentration diminishes with the prolongation of photocatalysis time, and the catalytic reaction rate of the samples begins to decelerate. After 4 h, it enters the late stage, where the catalytic reaction rate of the samples further slows down, resulting in a stable absorbance. It is important to note that under the remaining high concentration of methylene blue, some samples exhibited a stage where the absorbance began to stabilize. This observation indicates that, with increased illumination time, the photocatalysts in some samples experienced varying degrees of inactivation, which hindered the sustained regeneration of the samples. However, AC/CQDs/1.0TiO2 does not exhibit an overt equilibrium stage with the prolongation of illumination time, particularly in Fig. 7(c), which predominantly displays the first two stages, signifying its robust photocatalytic stability and favorable durable regeneration performance. In summary, the regeneration efficacy and durability of AC/CQDs/1.0TiO2 samples are commendable.

As illustrated in Fig. 7(d), the regeneration performance of the sample increases with the increase of pH value. This phenomenon can be attributed to the presence of positively charged groups within MB, which, at low pH values, struggle to adhere to the surface of the sample AC/CQDs/1.0TiO2 [46]. Consequently, the removal efficacy for MB is diminished, and the photocatalytic regeneration performance is correspondingly weak. Moreover, under conditions of high pH, the generated TiO2 is more stable and less prone to structural damage or inactivation. The dye solution also experiences an increase in hydroxyl group concentration at higher pH values, which fosters the generation of hydroxyl radicals, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic reaction and enhancing the regeneration performance of the sample [47]. In Table 3 , it can be observed that the AC/CQDs/1.0TiO2 sample in this study exhibits outstanding visible light photocatalytic regeneration performance in comparison to previous studies.

Following nitric acid oxidation, the original cell wall layer of the spent AC becomes thinner, and the resultant AC/CQDs exhibits a petal-like hierarchical structure, as depicted in Fig. 8(a). The original micropores are disrupted, with a substantial number collapsing, and fewer mesopores are retained, thereby diminishing its physical adsorption capabilities. Simultaneously, the oxidation with HNO3 introduces a multitude of oxygen-containing functional groups to the spent AC, which is conducive to chemical adsorption. Hence, the fundamental rationale for the regeneration of spent AC through the synthesis of CQDs lies in the formation of a plethora of oxygen-containing functional groups, and the predominant adsorption mechanism of the regenerated AC/CQDs has shifted from physical to chemical adsorption. In contrast to the AC not utilized for dye solution adsorption, this process recovered 68.17 % of its adsorption capacity.

The hydrothermally derived TiO2 is supported on the surface of AC/CQDs in the form of a thin film or partially granular structure, thickening the cell wall layer. Although the TiO2 support marginally increases the proportion of pores and oxygen-containing functional groups within the sample, it significantly reduces its oxygen content, resulting in a decrease in the total amount of oxygen-containing functional groups. Consequently, the adsorption performance of AC/CQDs/TiO2, which relies on chemical adsorption, is inferior to that of AC/CQDs.

The band gap value of AC/CQDs is 2.73 eV, rendering it poorly responsive to visible light, with a high efficiency of photoinduced electron-hole recombination, thus yielding low catalytic activity under visible light. With the loading of TiO2, as depicted in Fig. 8(b), TiO2 effectively leverages the photoluminescence characteristics of CQDs to bolster light absorption intensity and broaden the absorption spectrum of the composite. Secondly, the uniform TiO2 coating facilitates the transfer of photogenerated electrons to the AC/CQDs structure, where electrons transfer to the surface of AC, acting as an electron donor to capture oxygen (O2) and convert it into superoxide anion free radicals (O2−), while the holes generated by the composite material convert water (H2O) into ·OH. ·OH is a potent oxidizing agent for dye degradation, possessing sufficient potential to degrade MB dye molecules, and the entire process effectively curtails electron and hole recombination [50]. AC/CQDs/TiO2 possesses a unique mesoporous hierarchical structure. Nanoscale CQDs can infiltrate the mesopores of TiO2, and the specialized porous structure of TiO2 enables multi-level light reflection [51], thus enhancing light absorption efficiency. The upconversion effect of CQDs fluorescence enables the conversion of long-wavelength light into short-wavelength light, thereby augmenting the absorption intensity of AC/CQDs/TiO2 in the visible region. Electron coupling exists between the π orbital of CQDs and the conduction band of TiO2, inhibiting the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. Furthermore, the aromatic ring of methylene can form a π-π conjugated structure with the π orbital of CQDs, enhancing the adsorption of MB on the surface of the TiO2 photocatalyst [52], thereby improving the photocatalytic degradation effect.

In summary, the regeneration of AC/CQDs/TiO2 is attributable to the high catalytic activity of the photocatalyst within the composite system, which can convert the MB adsorbed by chemical adsorption on the surface into small molecules such as H2O and O2, re-release the adsorption sites, and restore the adsorption performance to achieve regeneration.

This study achieved the in-situ regeneration of spent AC through a chemical oxidation process that produced CQDs. The resulting CQDs, with a maximum quantum yield of 8 % and an average size of 3.87 nm, displayed yellow fluorescence when exposed to UV light. Meanwhile, the in-situ regenerated AC formed a mesoporous structure characterized by a unique petal-like morphology, enriched with oxygen-containing functional groups. Therefore, the regenerated AC/CQDs recovered 68.2 % of their adsorption capacity compared to the AC that was not used for dye solution adsorption, indicating a shift from physical to chemical adsorption mechanisms. Additionally, the incorporation of TiO2 preserved this unique structure while enhancing pore volume and increasing surface functionalities. This modification leaded to a reduced band gap of 2.13 eV for the AC/CQDs/TiO2 composite, thereby improving its responsiveness to visible light and minimizing electron-hole recombination. As a result, the in-situ regeneration of spent AC demonstrated exceptional photocatalytic regeneration performance under visible light, which initially increased and then decreased with the addition of TiCl4, ultimately outperforming findings from previous studies.

Wenjing Liu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Zihang Qiao: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Bin Wang: Investigation. Zheyu Li: Data curation. Minghui Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is supported by Natural Science Foundation of Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region (2021MS03046), National Natural Science Foundation of China (31960292), and Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region Key Laboratory Capacity Building Project (BR221017).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2024.111800.

He, Photodegradation performance and recyclability of a porous nitrogen and carbon co-doped TiO2/activated carbon composite prepared by an extremely fast one-step microwave method, RSC advances 6 (2016) 84457-84463.
